narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 34 (under the usual 36.7 average), low Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1 (far below averages around 5.5-5.7), lower-middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 68.4% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 decile ranking, in that order of impact. Your low Year 3 decile, placing you in the bottom tenth of your school's grades that year, was the strongest driver pushing the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model a significant drop or weakness in later academic performance compared to peers. Your LSAT score, while solid for many, worked in your favor here as it pulled the prediction somewhat toward passing, being closer to average than your other metrics. Similarly, your low Year 1 decile contributed toward the failure prediction but with less force than Year 3, highlighting early struggles that stood out against typical students who rank much higher. Overall, your academic deciles were notably lower than most applicants, which overshadowed positives like planning to study full-time, a path taken by over 90% of students in the data. This combination made your profile riskier than the strong, consistent performers the model favors for bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters that address your academic trajectory. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting schools with holistic reviews or bridge programs, and retaking the bar isn't uncommon even for admitted students. With targeted improvements, you can reshape your path to law school and bar passage.
